catalog_description: Event dispatcher patterns: declaring, broadcasting, binding across Blueprints, and handler wiring.
tags: event dispatcher delegate bind broadcast callback observer listener notify cross-blueprint publish subscribe

patterns.name: CrossBlueprint_DispatchAndBind
patterns.description: The publisher-subscriber pattern across Blueprints. The source Blueprint declares a dispatcher and broadcasts it via call_delegate when state changes. The listener Blueprint stores an object reference variable typed to the source, binds in Construct or BeginPlay via bind_dispatcher with a Target input pointing to that reference, and handles the event with a custom_event whose parameters match the dispatcher signature.
patterns.notes: Bind in Construct or BeginPlay, never Tick. The handler must be a custom_event (not a function) because dispatchers are delegate-based. Handler parameter names must match dispatcher parameter names exactly. The bind node's delegate pin auto-wires to the nearest compatible custom_event in the same plan. For explicit control, wire "Delegate": "@handler.OutputDelegate" in the bind step's inputs.

patterns.name: SameBlueprint_Dispatch
patterns.description: Declare and broadcast a dispatcher on the same Blueprint. Create the dispatcher with blueprint.add_event_dispatcher, then use call_delegate (or call_dispatcher -- they are interchangeable aliases) in plan_json to broadcast it.
patterns.notes: The dispatcher must exist before the plan runs. Broadcast passes parameter values to all bound listeners. This is the simplest dispatcher use case -- no Target input needed since the dispatcher lives on self.

patterns.name: SameBlueprint_Bind
patterns.description: Bind to a dispatcher on the same Blueprint, splitting handler logic into a separate entry point. Use bind_dispatcher with no Target input (self-context is implied) and a custom_event as the handler.
patterns.notes: Omit the Target input for self-context binding. This pattern is useful when the same Blueprint both dispatches and handles the event (e.g., a manager that reacts to its own state changes in a separate execution chain).

patterns.name: ComponentDelegate_vs_Dispatcher
patterns.description: Built-in component delegates (OnComponentBeginOverlap, OnComponentHit, OnComponentEndOverlap) are NOT event dispatchers. They use the event op with a component_name property, which resolves to UK2Node_ComponentBoundEvent via SCS inspection. bind_dispatcher is only for user-created event dispatchers.
patterns.notes: Do not use bind_dispatcher for component delegates -- it will fail to find them. Component delegates: {"op":"event","target":"OnComponentBeginOverlap","properties":{"component_name":"CollisionComp"}}. User dispatchers: {"op":"bind_dispatcher","target":"OnHealthChanged"}. The distinction is automatic if you use the correct op.

patterns.name: Gameplay_To_UI_Dispatcher
patterns.description: The preferred pattern for gameplay actors updating UI widgets. The gameplay actor declares a dispatcher and broadcasts when state changes. The widget binds in Construct using a reference to the gameplay actor and updates its TextBlock/ProgressBar/etc. in the custom_event handler via SetText or SetPercent. This decouples gameplay from presentation and allows multiple widgets to independently react to the same state change.
patterns.notes: The widget's Construct event is the correct bind point (not BeginPlay, which fires too early for widgets). The handler custom_event receives dispatcher params and calls SetText/SetPercent/SetVisibility on the widget's own components. Direct function calls from gameplay to widgets work but create tight coupling — prefer dispatchers when multiple consumers might need the data or when the widget should be independently swappable.
